original_abstract: Modern globalization, the international hierarchy of power, and most development frameworks remain tethered to the pursuit of economic growth. Yet, this reliance on Gross Domestic Product (GDP) is increasingly under fire for its social and ecological fallout. Rising global output is fundamentally linked to the overconsumption of energy and natural resources, threatening the very ecological systems that sustain life. In response, a diverse range of post-growth theories has emerged to offer alternative visions for the future. This article provides a comparative analysis of three influential schools of thought—steady-state economics, degrowth, and the well-being economy—tracing their intellectual origins, evolution, and real-world policy influence. By examining the tensions and synergies between these approaches, the paper seeks to advance the debate on how to effectively dismantle growth-centric paradigms in the international arena and move toward a system that prioritizes human and planetary health.
humanized_abstract: Current modern globalization, the global power structure, and development policies all continue to adhere to the principle of economic growth. Criticism of GDP and the social and ecological side effects of growth is increasingly prevalent. Current growth is always associated with the use of large amounts of energy and resources, which threaten our ecological systems. In response to the limitations of growth, post-growth theories have been developed that give an idea of the alternatives. In this article three alternative approaches are explained: steady-state economics, degrowth and the well-being economy. They will be discussed by means of an analysis of their origins, developments and influence on policy-making, in order to be able to highlight their differences and possible opportunities for cooperation and thus to further the global debate about how to dismantle the prevailing growth paradigm and create an economy that is sustainable for people and the planet.
